audienceSections.normal: In Minji and Junho’s morning routine, you will use [[grammar:N + 을/를 + verb]]. Put [[grammar:을]] after a consonant-ending object and [[grammar:를]] after a vowel-ending object, then put the verb at the end. 밥을 먹어요 means ‘eat a meal.’ Do not put the object particle on the person doing the action.
audienceSections.expert: This chapter presents [[grammar:N + 을/를 + verb]] in Minji and Junho’s morning routine. Object-particle allomorphy is coda-conditioned: 을 follows consonant-final 밥 and 책, while 를 follows vowel-final 커피 and 가방. Informal-polite predicates remain clause-final.

audienceSections.normal: Put [[grammar:을]] after a consonant-ending object and [[grammar:를]] after a vowel-ending object, then put the verb at the end. 밥을 먹어요 means ‘eat a meal.’ Do not put the object particle on the person doing the action.
audienceSections.expert: Object-particle allomorphy is coda-conditioned: 을 follows consonant-final 밥 and 책, while 를 follows vowel-final 커피 and 가방. Informal-polite predicates remain clause-final.

audienceSections.normal: Read each Hangul block as one syllable. Particles stay attached to the word before them, but the breakdown explains what each particle does.
audienceSections.expert: The examples use standard Korean in the informal-polite 해요 style. Particle allomorphy and contractions are analyzed as grammar while lexical entries retain citation forms.

breakdown.normal:
Read each exact korean line, then check its natural English meaning. Watch for [[grammar:N + 을/를 + verb]] where it appears.

- 아침에 민지는 물을 마셔요. — In the morning Minji drinks water.
- 그리고 밥을 먹어요. — Then she eats a meal.
- 책을 읽어요. — She reads a book.
- 준호는 커피를 마셔요. — Junho drinks coffee.
- 두 사람은 가방을 준비해요. — The two prepare their bags.
- 그리고 집에서 나가요. — Then they leave home.
breakdown.expert:
These exact reading lines provide the distributional evidence for [[grammar:N + 을/를 + verb]]. The grammar section above analyzes the relevant morphology and syntax.
